name: Cove Fort
short_description: BLM public rockhounding near Cove Fort in Millard County. Free collecting of agate, chalcedony, and jasper in a high desert terrain with minimal crowds.
description:
Cove Fort sits in the heart of Utah's Basin and Range province, a region known for abundant agate and chalcedony deposits. This BLM land offers rockhounders easy access to quality specimens without fees or permits, making it an excellent choice for beginners and families exploring Utah's mineral wealth.

The area around Cove Fort is characterized by exposed volcanic and sedimentary rock formations typical of the Sevier Plateau region. You will find scattered agate nodules and chalcedony fragments across the bajada surfaces and in shallow washes. The high desert environment means minimal vegetation, making mineral spotting straightforward even for first-time rockhounders.

Expect to walk the desert floor scanning for colorful specimens, particularly near wash beds where erosion exposes fresh material. The collecting is walk-and-pick rather than digging-intensive, and most productive areas lie within a short distance of parking pullouts. Many rockhounders report finding quality agate pieces suitable for display or lapidary work.

Being on BLM land means you have legal access year-round with simple rules: personal use only, 25-pound daily limit, and no motorized collection. The remoteness and lack of crowds make Cove Fort a peaceful alternative to more popular Utah rockhounding sites. Plan for at least a half-day outing to fully explore the productive zones.
history: Cove Fort sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and subsequent erosion created widespread silica-rich formations. Agate and chalcedony deposits here formed from silica-rich solutions during volcanic episodes 20-30 million years ago, and modern erosion continues to expose fresh nodules across the bajada surfaces.
tips: Search the desert floor and shallow washes where erosion has exposed agate nodules - the most productive areas are on gentle slopes facing south and southwest where weathering naturally exposes specimens. Bring a small classifier or sieve to help identify matrix rock versus solid agate; chalcedony feels waxy and smooth to the touch. Look for pieces with vibrant coloring - reds, oranges, and whites indicate quality material worth collecting. A UV light will reveal if specimens fluoresce, which can help confirm agate identity.
terrain_notes: High desert bajada with sparse sage brush and scattered rock outcrops. Elevation approximately 5,200-5,400 feet. Terrain is relatively flat with gentle slopes, scattered volcanic and sedimentary exposures, and multiple shallow washes cutting through the area. Walking is easy on hard-packed desert floor, though footing can be uneven with small rocks. Typical walking distance from parking to productive collecting zones is 0.25 to 1 mile.
best_season: October - April. The high desert around Cove Fort becomes extremely hot (95-105 degrees F) from May through September, making summer collecting uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Fall and winter offer cool, pleasant conditions ideal for extended field time. Spring (March-April) is also viable but less comfortable than fall.
written_directions: From Millard County, take US 50 east toward Cove Fort. The town of Cove Fort is located directly on US 50. Look for BLM land access points and small dirt pullouts along and near US 50 in the vicinity of the historical Cove Fort site. GPS coordinates 38.607968, -112.571855 mark the general collecting area. Parking is informal at pullouts; no developed parking lot exists.
road_conditions: US 50 is paved highway. Access to BLM collecting areas may involve short distances on graded dirt roads or direct pullout parking on highway shoulders. Passenger vehicles are adequate.
parking_notes: Informal pullout parking along US 50 near Cove Fort. No developed parking lot or facilities. Space is limited but usually available given the low usage of this site.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. All collection must be non-destructive surface gathering. Leave the area as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Non-destructive surface gathering only
nearest_services: Cove Fort is directly on US 50 with a historical site. Nearest full services (gas, food, lodging) are in Richfield (approximately 25 miles north) and Salina (approximately 15 miles south).
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat high desert terrain accessible to most hikers and rockhounders. Informal pullout parking on US 50 provides easy access. No wheelchair accessibility to collecting areas. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp volcanic rock and loose surface material. Walking distances of 0.25 to 1 mile from parking are typical.

faq.question: What types of agates and chalcedony can you find rockhounding at Cove Fort, Utah?
faq.answer: Cove Fort BLM land hosts quality agate and chalcedony specimens ranging from small fragments to hand-sized nodules. Most pieces feature earth-tone colors including reds, oranges, whites, and tans typical of the Basin and Range volcanic formations in this region. Chalcedony is abundant as solid nodules and banded material. Quality is suitable for display, lapidary work, or collection.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Cove Fort free and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Cove Fort is completely free. No permits or fees are required since this is BLM public land. You simply need to follow the standard BLM collection rules: personal use only, 25-pound daily limit, and non-destructive surface gathering. You can rockhound year-round without advance notice or permission.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Millard County at Cove Fort?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for rockhounding at Cove Fort. Temperatures are cool and comfortable (50-70 degrees F), allowing extended time in the field without heat stress. Summer months (May-September) are too hot and potentially dangerous for safe collecting. Spring is also viable but fall offers the most pleasant conditions.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Cove Fort for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Cove Fort is one of Utah's most beginner-friendly rockhounding locations. The terrain is flat and open, walking distances are short, and agate nodules are visible and easy to spot on the desert surface. No special skills or tools are required - just walk and pick. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably. The lack of crowds and simple BLM rules make it perfect for first-time rockhounders.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do you need for rockhounding at Cove Fort, Utah?
faq.answer: For Cove Fort collecting, bring sturdy hiking boots, sun protection (hat, sunscreen, sunglasses), and plenty of water. A small backpack, 5-gallon bucket, or collection bag holds your daily limit of 25 pounds. Optional but helpful: a hand lens for examining chalcedony quality, a small classifier or screen to separate matrix from specimens, and a UV light to test for fluorescence. A geological hammer is useful but not essential since surface specimens are easily collected.

faq.question: How far is Cove Fort rockhounding from Salina and other Utah towns with services?
faq.answer: Cove Fort is directly on US 50, approximately 15 miles south of Salina and 25 miles south of Richfield. Both towns offer gas, food, lodging, and supplies. The location is accessible as a day trip from central Utah. Cove Fort itself has a historical monument but minimal modern services, so plan to bring supplies from nearby towns.

meta_title: Cove Fort Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Chalcedony
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Cove Fort, Utah. Collect agate, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Beginner-friendly near Salina.
